sources.notes: Read the full operation, examples, and constraints: n <= 14 and the sum of 2^n over tests is at most 2^14.

The official tutorial page exposes the accepted reference code. It confirms four-boundary inclusion-exclusion, exact-box one-galaxy counts, an interval-plus-column-mask decomposition DP, and a final weighted component-count DP.
Detailed independent write-up and code used to cross-check the bounding-rectangle merge theorem and the alternative f/g/h rectangle-decomposition DP.
Independent Chinese study note confirming that terminal components have disjoint row and column projections and outlining the same generating-rectangle f/g/h recurrence.

Re-proved both directions of the rectangle-merging characterization. Exhaustive legal-operation search matched it for every star set through 4x4; a direct translation of the official recurrence matched brute subset sums for every matrix through n=3 and 50 random sparse n=4 matrices, including sample 4.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu solution-index candidate; usable article content was not exposed during this review, so it was not promoted to sources.

annotation.summary: For every nonempty subset of the 1-cells of an n x n grid, repeatedly add any star that would have at least three direct row/column neighbors, take the minimum attainable number of connected galaxies, and sum these values modulo 1e9+7.
annotation.constraints: t <= 100, 1 <= n <= 14, and sum(2^n) <= 2^14. A chosen input subset ranges over as many as n^2 available stars, so enumerating subsets of stars is impossible; the exponential dimension must be only one grid axis.
annotation.core_idea: Represent every current galaxy by the axis-aligned bounding rectangle of its stars. Along a connected galaxy, the projections of its direct-edge segments cover its full x- and y-intervals. In any legal insertion, among at least three directional neighbors there is an opposite pair; those two stars were already directly connected before insertion, while the third neighbor's component shares the insertion coordinate, so the insertion only merges components whose bounding rectangles overlap in x or in y. Conversely, if two distinct components overlap in x, an extremal x-coordinate of one lies strictly inside the other's x-span; projection coverage gives a horizontal direct edge of the latter crossing that vertical line, and inserting at the crossing has two horizontal neighbors plus a vertical neighbor from the former component. Thus it is legal and merges them; the y case is symmetric. Therefore the value is exactly the number of rectangles left after repeatedly merging any pair with an overlapping x- or y-projection, and distinct terminal rectangles are disjoint in both projections. Let one[l][r][u][v] count selected subsets that end as one galaxy with exact bounding box [l,r) x [u,v). First count all nonempty subsets with that exact box by inclusion-exclusion over its four boundary sides. Process row spans by length and use a column mask DP to count and subtract exact-box selections that split into at least two terminal rectangles: append the last rectangle in row order only when its contiguous column interval is disjoint from the previous mask. Finally scan rows with ways[x][mask] and sum[x][mask]; append any one-galaxy box starting at x whose column interval is unused, and update sum by (sum + ways) times one because every appended box contributes one galaxy.
annotation.complexity: O(n^4 * 2^n) time and O(n^2 * 2^n + n^4) memory per test case. The zero-run interval enumerations total O(n * 2^n) over all masks, rather than O(n^2 * 2^n).
tricks: compress the insertion closure to repeated unions of axis-aligned component bounding boxes
prove terminal component rectangles are disjoint on both coordinate axes
count subsets with an exact bounding box by inclusion-exclusion over four sides
order components by row interval and store used column projections as an n-bit mask
carry both the number of constructions and the sum of component counts in the final DP
enumerate contiguous intervals contained in zero-runs of a mask with O(n * 2^n) aggregate cost
main_agent_review_notes: Algorithm and recurrence are high-confidence after official-code, independent-write-up, exhaustive closure, and brute-sum cross-checks. The official tutorial prose was lazy-loaded, so the official source contribution was its reference implementation rather than a prose proof.
Taxonomy review requested for proposed candidate math/geometry/bounding-box. If rejected, replace that tag with existing math/geometry; no other new tag is proposed.

solution_variants.name: official-exact-box-interval-mask-dp
solution_variants.summary: Use 16-term boundary inclusion-exclusion to count selections with each exact box. In increasing row-span order, derive one-galaxy counts by subtracting canonical multi-rectangle decompositions maintained by a used-column bitmask. A second row scan appends disjoint one-galaxy rectangles and maintains both the number of selected subsets and their total galaxy count.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^4 * 2^n) time and O(n^2 * 2^n + n^4) memory.

solution_variants.name: explicit-f-g-h-rectangle-decomposition
solution_variants.summary: Let f be the number of one-galaxy selections with a fixed exact rectangle. Compute it from all nonempty selections in that rectangle by subtracting selections with a canonically first proper component; a g[l][r][mask] DP counts legal collections of later component rectangles in remaining rows and disjoint columns. Run the same scan with h storing the total component count, and return h on the full grid.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^8 + n^4 * 2^n) time and O(n^4 + n^2 * 2^n) memory.

tags.description: Reduce a planar point set or connected component to its minimum axis-aligned bounding rectangle and reason from its four extrema or coordinate projections; use when this rectangle preserves the relevant merge, feasibility, or objective information.
tags.created_reason: Generic geometry does not expose the decisive and reusable bounding-box closure invariant: galaxies merge exactly through overlap of one coordinate projection, and the full operation history can be discarded after taking rectangle unions.
tags.evidence: The central proof replaces each galaxy by its minimum axis-aligned rectangle and proves that a legal insertion is equivalent to merging two such rectangles whose x- or y-projections overlap.
The value of each selected subset is a minimum connected-component count, and the decisive theorem characterizes exactly which current components can be merged by future insertions.
Both the one-galaxy subtraction DP and the final weighted DP use an n-bit mask for the union of column intervals already occupied by row-ordered terminal rectangles.
The states count selected star subsets, and the final paired states maintain both their multiplicity and the sum of their numbers of terminal galaxies.

After all possible merges, two distinct component rectangles overlap in neither axis; this permits row ordering and requires each appended column interval to lie wholly in a zero-run of the used mask.
The helper DP processes row intervals [l,r) by increasing length and stores exact column intervals [u,v) for each candidate one-galaxy bounding box.
A 16-term inclusion-exclusion over deleting any subset of the four boundary sides converts 2^{count}-1 inside a rectangle into the number of nonempty selections with that exact bounding box.
The requested answer aggregates a structural weight over every nonempty subset of up to n^2 available stars, and the DP groups these subsets by terminal rectangle decomposition.

proposed_new_tags.description: Reduce a planar point set or connected component to its minimum axis-aligned bounding rectangle and reason from its four extrema or coordinate projections; use when this rectangle preserves the relevant merge, feasibility, or objective information.
proposed_new_tags.created_reason: This classic representation is substantially more searchable than generic geometry and is the indispensable closure reduction in CF2062H; it is also reusable in extremal-coordinate and component-merging problems.
